title: Computer and Information Research Scientists
keyDrivers: These scientists work at the frontier of computing, tackling genuinely novel problems where pattern-matching from existing solutions fails, the kind of deep, creative reasoning that AI struggles to replicate. They are also the people most likely to be designing, directing, and evaluating AI systems themselves, making their expertise more valuable as AI expands. However, the role lacks physical or legal protections, meaning its resilience depends entirely on staying ahead of the technology curve.

taskData.full: Analyze problems to develop solutions involving computer hardware and software.
Conduct logical analyses of business, scientific, engineering, and other technical problems, formulating mathematical models of problems for solution by computers.
Assign or schedule tasks to meet work priorities and goals.
Apply theoretical expertise and innovation to create or apply new technology, such as adapting principles for applying computers to new uses.
Consult with users, management, vendors, and technicians to determine computing needs and system requirements.
Design computers and the software that runs them.
Develop and interpret organizational goals, policies, and procedures.
Meet with managers, vendors, and others to solicit cooperation and resolve problems.
Maintain network hardware and software, direct network security measures, and monitor networks to ensure availability to system users.
Participate in multidisciplinary projects in areas such as virtual reality, human-computer interaction, or robotics.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: AI coding assistants and automated data pipelines are absorbing the literature review, data processing, and boilerplate model evaluation work that junior researchers spend most of their time on — compressing the entry-level pipeline into the field [Anthropic / O*NET, Jan 2026]. Tech industry research lab headcount has contracted sharply since 2022, with major AI labs consolidating teams and shifting from basic research toward applied product work, narrowing the number of academic-style research positions available in industry [Layoffs.fyi, Jan 2025].
risks.statLabel: tech research and engineering roles cut at major AI labs in 2023–2024

opportunities.body: Original hypothesis generation, experimental design, and peer-reviewed publication require scientific judgment that remains structurally resistant to automation — AI accelerates the execution layer but cannot replace the researcher directing it [Anthropic / O*NET, Jan 2026]. The volume of AI research published has grown dramatically, creating sustained demand for scientists who can evaluate, critique, and extend the frontier: reviewers, collaborators, and principal investigators who can direct AI-assisted research programs at scale [Layoffs.fyi, Jan 2025].
opportunities.statLabel: increase in AI-related research papers published since 2020

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The research work most protected from displacement is the part that requires genuine originality - identifying novel problems, designing experiments that test real unknowns, and interpreting ambiguous results in context of the field. Start using AI tools to accelerate the literature review and initial modeling phases, which frees more time for the creative and judgment-intensive work at the frontier [Anthropic / O*NET, Jan 2026]. Over the next year, build depth in AI research itself or in domain-specific applications of machine learning, since those intersections carry both higher demand and stronger protections than generalist computer science research.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Doctoral degrees are the standard credential for this field, and most positions are in universities, government labs, and the research divisions of large technology companies [U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Sep 2025]. The path is long but the role's structural protections are strong - original research output is among the hardest things for AI to generate reliably, and the judgment required to identify what is worth investigating is a persistent human advantage. Build a research portfolio that shows systems-level thinking and the ability to publish findings, not just implement existing methods.

quotes.quote: AI is accelerating the literature review and hypothesis generation phases of research, but the most critical bottleneck -- designing experiments that test genuinely novel questions -- remains human. Research scientists who are using AI most effectively are front-loading their own judgment: deciding what to test before the AI generates the analysis.

AI usage data shows that computer science research tasks are among the most augmented in the entire economy -- meaning AI is extending what researchers can do rather than replacing the researchers. The pattern is human-directed AI execution: the scientist decides the question; the AI runs the experiment variants.

Private sector AI research roles now offer salaries and compute resources that university labs cannot match, and the gap is widening. The candidates moving into those positions are those with both strong publication records and demonstrated fluency with large-scale ML infrastructure -- not one or the other.

The occupational data shows research scientists directing AI systems at the highest rates of any profession -- the research scientist's primary interaction with AI is as a tool user and system overseer, not as someone whose work AI replaces. That positions the role better than almost any other in the face of automation pressure.

sources.name: Anthropic / O*NET
sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index - Task-level automation and augmentation estimates for Computer and Information Research Scientists

sources.name: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics

sources.name: ACM Queue

careerCluster.title: Information Systems Manager
careerCluster.fit: You already solve complex problems, coordinate with stakeholders, and assign tasks, but this move is a step up into full ownership of budgets, staff, and department operations rather than research outputs.
steps: Earn PMP or CISSP certification
Take on team lead or project manager role
Shadow a CIS manager for 3-6 months

careerCluster.title: Computer Network Architect
careerCluster.fit: Your background in analyzing complex systems and designing technical solutions carries over directly, but the job shifts from research and innovation to hands-on network operations, maintenance, and day-to-day infrastructure management.
steps: Earn Cisco CCNA or CompTIA Network+ certification
Build a home lab running real network configurations
Study firewall tools: pfSense, Cisco ASA, or Palo Alto

careerCluster.title: Data Scientist
careerCluster.fit: A computer and information research scientist moving to data science is a step down in credential and abstraction — the mathematical and statistical foundations carry over entirely, but the job shifts from advancing theory and designing systems to applying existing methods to business data.
steps: No new credential required
Build applied ML/data portfolio in Python (pandas, scikit-learn)
Get familiar with business data tooling: SQL, Tableau, or dbt

careerCluster.title: IT Project Manager
careerCluster.fit: A computer and information research scientist moving to IT project management is a step down in technical depth — your systems and problem-solving background helps you communicate with engineers, but the job shifts from doing technical work to coordinating it.
steps: Earn PMP or PMI-ACP certification
No programming work required in role
Build experience managing cross-functional technical teams

careerCluster.title: Computer Systems Architect
careerCluster.fit: A computer and information research scientist moving to systems engineering is a step down from research to applied work — your deep technical foundations carry over, but the job shifts from advancing knowledge to designing and operating production systems.
steps: No new credential required
Earn AWS or Azure Solutions Architect certification
Build hands-on systems deployment portfolio

careerCluster.title: Information Security Engineer
steps: Earn CompTIA Security+ to establish baseline security credential
Study network security and threat modeling (OWASP, STRIDE) via hands-on labs (TryHackMe or HackTheBox)
Build a portfolio project: document a full vulnerability assessment of a web application or network segment
careerCluster.fit: Computer science researchers who study systems, networks, or AI safety are building the foundational knowledge that security engineering requires. The shift is from publishing findings to deploying defenses — the analytical rigor transfers directly.

emergingCareers.title: AI Safety Researcher
emergingCareers.description: Studies failure modes, alignment properties, and societal risks of large AI systems. Designs and runs experiments on model behavior under distribution shift, adversarial prompting, and reward misspecification. Publishes findings that inform how labs build safer, more predictable AI systems.

stat.text: AI and Machine Learning Specialists rank as the single fastest-growing job globally through 2030, with demand expected to grow 40% — driven in part by regulatory requirements that are mandating dedicated safety and alignment functions at AI labs.

emergingCareers.fit: Your research methodology and comfort with formal models of computation transfer directly — alignment work is applied math and empirical science, and your background in CS theory gives you stronger foundations than most safety candidates.
steps: Complete ARENA (Alignment Research Engineer Accelerator) or BlueDot Impact's AI Safety Fundamentals course
Replicate one paper from the mechanistic interpretability literature (e.g. Anthropic's circuit findings) and publish your replication notes publicly
Apply to an AI safety research fellowship: MATS, SPAR, or Anthropic's residency program

emergingCareers.title: ML Research Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Bridges the gap between research and production by implementing, reproducing, and scaling machine learning experiments. Owns the engineering infrastructure that lets research scientists iterate faster — training pipelines, experiment tracking, compute orchestration, and model evaluation frameworks.

stat.text: AI and ML roles are the fastest-growing segment of the technical labor market, with a 28% salary premium over adjacent software engineering roles and open position counts up 35% year-over-year as of early 2025.
stat.sourceTitle: The Generative AI Job Market 2025: Data & Insights

emergingCareers.search_query: "ML research engineer" OR "machine learning engineer" research jobs 2025
emergingCareers.fit: Your ability to read papers and understand algorithms at a deep level is the hard part — the engineering skills (GPU programming, distributed training) are learnable, and most research labs will train you on infrastructure if you can engage with the math.
steps: Reproduce a recent NeurIPS or ICML paper end-to-end using PyTorch, documenting every discrepancy from the paper's reported results
Complete fast.ai's Practical Deep Learning for Coders and Jeremy Howard's course on training from scratch to build hands-on intuition
Set up a personal Weights & Biases project tracking 10+ experiment runs to demonstrate you understand the research engineering workflow

faqs.question: Is computer science research a viable career when AI itself is doing more research tasks?
faqs.answer: Computer Science Researcher scores 63/100 on AI exposure, in solid territory, with 3,200 annual openings and 20% projected growth. AI tools are absorbing literature review, data processing, and code generation that previously consumed research time, which frees researchers to focus on original hypothesis formation and experimental design. The Stanford HAI AI Index 2024 found academic and industry AI research output growing faster than the number of researchers, which suggests the field is expanding rather than contracting despite automation of supporting tasks.

faqs.question: What does a computer science researcher earn, across academia and industry?
faqs.answer: The median salary is $140,910, but research scientists at major AI labs typically earn $200,000 to $400,000 or more in total compensation, with significant variation by seniority and location. Academic positions, including tenure-track faculty, typically range from $100,000 to $180,000 depending on the institution and specialization. Government research labs offer salaries between academia and industry, with added job security and mission-driven work that attracts researchers who prioritize applied national interest problems.

faqs.question: What research tasks is AI handling, and what remains the domain of human scientists?
faqs.answer: AI tools now handle systematic literature review, code generation for experiments, and data pipeline construction faster than any individual researcher. Where human researchers remain essential is forming the research question, designing experiments that can actually test a hypothesis, and interpreting results in ways that advance scientific understanding rather than optimize a metric. Peer-reviewed publication, which requires defending methodology and contributing to scientific discourse, is not substitutable by current AI systems.

faqs.question: What credentials and skills matter most for a career in computer science research?
faqs.answer: A PhD is effectively required for independent research positions at universities and for most staff research scientist roles at major AI labs. Publications in top venues, NeurIPS, ICML, CVPR, ICLR, carry more weight than GPA or institutional prestige in most research hiring. Researchers who combine theoretical depth with the ability to implement and benchmark systems, not just propose ideas, are the profile that gets hired into the highest-impact and best-compensated positions.
